title: Prepositions of Place, Time, and Movement: A Parent's Toolkit
whatIsIt: Prepositions are small words that show relationships — where something is, when something happens, or how things move. Words like 'in', 'on', 'at', 'under', 'through', and 'between' may be small, but they're absolutely essential for clear communication. Without prepositions, we'd never know if the cat was on the table or under it! Children often learn prepositions best through physical experience and visual contexts.
howToHelp: Play 'Simon Says' with prepositions: 'Simon says put your hand ON your head, UNDER the table, BETWEEN your knees!' Physical movement creates strong memory anchors.
Create a preposition obstacle course at home: crawl UNDER chairs, jump OVER pillows, walk AROUND the sofa. Learning through the body is powerful.
Use a toy and a box to demonstrate every preposition you can think of: in, on, under, behind, next to, in front of, between. Let your child place the toy and say the sentence.
Point out time prepositions in daily routines: 'We eat breakfast AT 7 o'clock, ON Monday mornings, IN the summer.' Repetition in real contexts builds mastery.
Draw simple maps together and describe routes using movement prepositions: 'Go past the park, turn at the corner, walk along the street.'
Read picture books that emphasize location words — many children's books are built around preposition concepts (like 'Where's Spot?').
commonMistakes: 'I go to school in Monday' instead of 'on Monday' — time prepositions are often learned by pattern, and 'in' gets overused.
'The cat is in the table' instead of 'under the table' — spatial prepositions require understanding of the physical relationship, not just memorization.
'I live at London' instead of 'in London' — larger places use 'in', while specific addresses use 'at'.
Omitting prepositions entirely: 'I put the book the table' — this often happens when children are focused on vocabulary and grammar but haven't yet internalized preposition habits.
signsOfProgress: Uses place prepositions accurately in spontaneous play: 'The dinosaur is behind the couch!'
Corrects themselves when describing locations: 'No wait, it's UNDER the bed, not on it.'
Naturally uses time prepositions without prompting: 'I'll see you on Saturday.'
Understands that the same preposition can have different meanings: 'on the table' (surface) versus 'on Monday' (day).
Begins using more advanced prepositions: 'through', 'across', 'along', 'towards' in storytelling.
ifStruggling: Focus on ONE category at a time — place prepositions first, then time, then movement. Don't mix them until each feels solid.
Use real objects and real spaces. Abstract explanations rarely work for prepositions — children need to feel the spatial relationships.
Accept approximation at first: 'in the box' and 'inside the box' are both correct. Precision comes with time.
Make it a scavenger hunt: 'Find something under your bed, on your desk, and next to your window.'
Remember that prepositions are one of the most arbitrary parts of English — even advanced learners make mistakes with them. Be patient!
Praise spatial thinking, not just correct words: 'I love how you thought about where the toy was hiding!'
